title: useBoardInteraction()/injectBoardInteraction() return undefined silently — the HARD-RULE interaction channel fails with no diagnostic
evidence: useBoardInteraction.ts:393-395 `export function useBoardInteraction(): BoardInteraction | undefined { return inject(BOARD_INTERACTION_KEY); }` and useActionController.ts:1576-1578 `injectBoardInteraction()` just forwards it. Contrast with the sibling injectActionController.ts:1507-1512 which DOES throw: `if (!controller) { throw new Error('injectActionController() must be called inside a GameShell context'); }`. Real game UIs are forced to litter guards: hex/src/ui/components/HexBoard.vue:101 `if (!boardInteraction) return false;` and checkers/src/ui/components/CheckersBoard.vue repeatedly `if (boardInteraction) {...}` / `boardInteraction?.clear()`.
whyConcern: A HARD RULE states 'All UI interactions must work in a Custom UI and Action Panel in parity ... through useBoardInteraction.' Yet the entry point to that channel returns undefined when used outside a GameShell/provider, so highlighting, selection, and drag-drop all silently no-op (every isSelectable/isHighlighted returns false) with zero error to explain why. The inconsistency with injectActionController (which throws) means two sibling APIs have opposite failure modes.
fixPaths: Provide a throwing variant matching injectActionController (e.g. injectBoardInteraction throws an actionable 'must be used inside <GameShell>' error), and keep an explicit `tryUseBoardInteraction()` for the optional case. useBoardInteraction.ts:393, useActionController.ts:1576.
verifierReasoning: Verified all cited code. useBoardInteraction.ts:393-395 returns inject(BOARD_INTERACTION_KEY) with no default/throw, so it yields undefined silently outside a provider. injectBoardInteraction (useActionController.ts:1576-1578) merely forwards it. The sibling injectActionController (useActionController.ts:1507-1513) DOES throw 'injectActionController() must be called inside a GameShell context' — confirming two sibling injection APIs have opposite failure modes. Game UIs do litter guards: hex HexBoard.vue:101 `if (!boardInteraction) return false;` and checkers CheckersBoard.vue uses `boardInteraction?.clear()` / `if (boardInteraction)` at lines 376, 397, 433, 440, 447, 454, 580. So the inconsistency and silent-undefined behavior are factually real. However the severity is somewhat overstated: GameShell.vue:444 always calls provideBoardInteraction, so in correct usage (inside GameShell) the channel never fails — undefined only occurs on misuse outside a GameShell. The HARD RULE about parity is not actually broken for shipping games; the real cost is defensive-guard boilerplate plus a missing diagnostic on misuse. That's a genuine DX/API-consistency problem worth fixing (mirror injectActionController by throwing, or provide a sensible default), but it does not break correctly-used code, so low rather than medium.

title: MCTS incremental undo silently degrades to a full game rebuild per iteration for card games
evidence: undoCommands rolls back each iteration (mcts-bot.ts:662,690); on failure recoverFromUndoFailure (1253-1258) does restoreGame (new GameClass + loadSerializedState + restoreFlowState, 1214-1247). inverse.ts returns null for SHUFFLE(70-72), CREATE(58-65), REMOVE(67), SET_VISIBILITY(74-79), so undo fails and backprop recovers and returns (663-671).
whyConcern: Card games (Go Fish, Cribbage) deal, shuffle, flip, discard constantly, so most playouts force a full reconstruction every iteration, 10-100x slower, blowing the search timeout or Worker CPU; the advertised incremental design (24-31) reverts to reconstruct-per-iteration.
verifierReasoning:
The underlying mechanism exists. undoCommands (game.ts:944-949) stops and returns false on the first non-invertible command (via undoLastCommand, which fails when _inverseHistory entry is null, lines 921-925). createInverseCommand (inverse.ts:50-113) returns null for SHUFFLE (70-72), CREATE (58-61), CREATE_MANY (63-65), ADD_VISIBLE_TO (77-79), START_GAME, END_GAME. On undo failure, backpropagateWithUndo (mcts-bot.ts:662-671, 690-697) calls recoverFromUndoFailure (1253-1258), which does a full restoreGame (new GameClass + loadSerializedState + restoreFlowState, 1214-1247). So a playout that crosses a SHUFFLE does force a full rebuild that iteration, defeating the incremental design. That much is genuinely present.

However, the finding's evidence is substantially WRONG, which inflates its severity:
1. The claim asserts REMOVE returns null at line 67. FALSE — inverse.ts:67-68 is `case 'REMOVE': return createRemoveInverse(game, command);`, which returns a valid MOVE inverse (164-182). Discards/removes are invertible.
2. The claim asserts SET_VISIBILITY returns null at 74-79. FALSE — inverse.ts:74-75 is `case 'SET_VISIBILITY': return createSetVisibilityInverse(...)`, a valid inverse (188-200). The author conflated it with ADD_VISIBLE_TO (77-79). Card flips are invertible.
3. MOVE (draw/deal of an existing card) is invertible (119-136).

So the most common card operations the title leans on — deal, flip, discard, draw — are all invertible. The only realistic mid-playout trigger is SHUFFLE (CREATE happens at setup, before the search root, not during playouts). SHUFFLE during a playout only occurs at reshuffle / new-round deals, which for a depth-capped playout (playoutDepth, mcts-bot.ts:572) may not even be reached. So "most playouts force a full reconstruction every iteration" is unsupported.

Impact is also bounded: when recovery fires, the cost is one restoreGame per affected iteration — essentially equivalent to the clone-per-iteration baseline that non-incremental MCTS uses anyway. The "10-100x slower" figure is unsubstantiated; this is graceful degradation to baseline, not catastrophic. The recovery path is also functionally correct (state-authoritative restore + rootCommandCount reset), so there is no correctness bug.

Real mechanism, but the supporting facts are partly incorrect and the severity/frequency are exaggerated. Genuine but minor — a lost optimization for shuffle-heavy multi-round games, not a high-severity defect.

title: Debug time-travel/rewind uses the replay path the codebase itself declares 'unsound' — silently shows WRONG historical state
evidence: src/session/state-history.ts:114 `GameRunner.replay<G>(...)` in getStateAtAction and :428 in rewindToAction. But src/runtime/runner.ts:291-298 says of replay: "The previous replay-based restore was ... also unsound: selection-step / pending-completed actions are recorded in NEITHER command nor action history, so replaying an incomplete actionHistory mis-positioned the flow and crashed real games". The same file's undoToTurnStart (state-history.ts:347-371) and stateless-ops.ts:367-374 both explicitly DON'T replay and restore from checkpoints instead, with the comment: "Replay re-runs start() + recorded actions, which never re-applies pending/selection mutations (Piece.putInto, recorded in neither command nor action history) ... it loses prior-turn equipment and mis-positions the flow". getStateAtAction is exposed live via game-session.ts:772.
whyConcern: Within a single subsystem, undo restores authoritatively from checkpoints while time-travel/diff/rewind replay actions. The replay path is documented elsewhere as producing wrong state and crashing. So the debug panel's time-travel and state-diff can show a board that never actually existed, and rewind can resurrect a corrupted/mis-positioned flow — the exact failure class fromSnapshot was written to eliminate, still live in the debugger.
fixPaths: Route getStateAtAction/getStateDiff/rewindToAction through the same checkpoint-based restore (GameRunner.fromSnapshot + actionCheckpoints) that undoToTurnStart and stateless-ops already use; delete the replay calls in state-history.ts. Keep one restore primitive.
verifierReasoning:
Verified all citations against source. The finding is factually accurate. state-history.ts:114 (getStateAtAction) and :428 (rewindToAction) call GameRunner.replay, while undoToTurnStart (state-history.ts:354-371) and stateless-ops.ts handleUndo (367-390) deliberately AVOID replay and restore authoritatively from actionCheckpoints via fromSnapshot. runner.ts:280-301 explicitly documents replay as "unsound": pending/selection mutations (Piece.putInto) are recorded in neither command nor action history, so replaying an incomplete actionHistory mis-positions the flow and crashed real games (the exact bug class fromSnapshot was written to eliminate). getStateDiff (state-history.ts:166-167) composes two getStateAtAction calls, so it inherits the same unsoundness. These are reachable live: server/handlers/games.ts:248/271/902 and worker/index.ts:1164/1190.

The inconsistency is genuine AND fixable with existing infrastructure: actionCheckpoints[k] (runner.ts:64-112) holds authoritative state at every action index in a live session, so time-travel/rewind could use fromSnapshot(checkpoints[k]) just like undo does. This is a real architecture-level intra-subsystem contradiction.

Impact assessment tempering the severity: (1) These are debug/dev-only features (time-travel debugging, state-diff highlighting, rewind explicitly "intended for debug/development use"). (2) The read paths (getStateAtAction/getStateDiff) are wrapped in try/catch and return {success:false} on throw — they cannot crash the live session; worst case they show wrong/missing historical state. (3) The wrongness only manifests for games that use pending/selection mutations (equipment via Piece.putInto, selection-step mutations); simpler games whose entire state lives in command/action history replay correctly. rewindToAction is the most serious because it replaces the live runner, saves, and broadcasts — for affected games it can corrupt/mis-position the live dev game — but it is still a debug-gated operation, not normal gameplay.

So: real correctness/architecture bug, accurately described ("silently shows WRONG historical state"; rewind can resurrect a mis-positioned flow), but confined to debug/dev tooling with non-destructive, error-caught read paths. That makes "high" slightly overstated; medium is the appropriate independent severity.

title: 'Who can act' is represented two ways (awaitingPlayers[] vs currentPlayer) and re-branched in 9 files
evidence: The same if/else appears verbatim in many consumers: stateless-ops.ts selectDueAISeat:171-188 and handleDebugActionTraces:625-636; state-history.ts getActionTraces:281-293; plus the field is consumed across src/ai/mcts-bot.ts, src/engine/element/game.ts, src/engine/utils/snapshot.ts, src/engine/flow/engine.ts, src/session/utils.ts, src/session/ai-controller.ts (grep: 9 non-test files reference awaitingPlayers). Pattern: `if (flowState.awaitingPlayers && awaitingPlayers.length > 0) { find player... } else { use currentPlayer }`.
whyConcern: There is no single canonical 'can seat N act now?' predicate; the simultaneous-vs-sequential turn invariant is re-derived everywhere. Any consumer that checks only currentPlayer (forgetting awaitingPlayers) silently mishandles simultaneous-action phases, and vice versa.
fixPaths: Centralize one predicate on FlowState/engine, e.g. `flowState.canAct(seat): boolean` and `flowState.dueSeats(): number[]`, and replace every ad-hoc branch with it. Make currentPlayer/awaitingPlayers internal to the flow engine.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed by reading the cited code. FlowState carries two representations of "who can act": awaitingPlayers[] (simultaneous) and currentPlayer (sequential), defined at src/engine/flow/types.ts:252, produced by src/engine/flow/engine.ts. The "awaitingPlayers first, else currentPlayer" branch is genuinely re-derived across consumers: src/session/stateless-ops.ts:171-188 and 625-636; src/session/state-history.ts:281-293 (nearly verbatim copy of stateless-ops.ts:625-636, both computing isMyTurn + flowAllowedActions); src/session/utils.ts:28-31 and 369-374; src/session/ai-controller.ts:90-104 and 127-128; src/ai/mcts-bot.ts:744-766/849-886. So the duplication claim holds.

Two corrections to the finding's framing: (1) Canonical predicates DO exist — Game.canPlayerAct() at src/engine/element/game.ts:1682-1694 and isPlayersTurn() at src/session/utils.ts:24-35 — but they are themselves duplicates of each other and only return a boolean, so consumers needing flowAllowedActions re-inline anyway. (2) The 'silent mishandling' risk is theoretical: every consumer I inspected correctly checks awaitingPlayers before falling back to currentPlayer, and several of the 9 files (types.ts, engine.ts, snapshot.ts) are engine internals that define/produce the state rather than re-branch it, so the consumer count is inflated.

Net: a genuine duplication/drift maintainability issue (verbatim-duplicated derivation logic + two redundant boolean predicates + a duplicated AI-seat selector) with no demonstrated correctness bug. The two-representation split is also partly intentional state-machine design. Real but lower impact than medium; rate low.

title: Flow variables and forEach loop bindings use untyped magic-string keys (ctx.get/set, forEach `as`) — typos silently return undefined
evidence: flow/types.ts:62-64 `set: (name: string, value: unknown) => void; get: <T = unknown>(name: string) => T | undefined;`. builders.ts:446-454 setVar stores by string; builders.ts:178-193 forEach takes `as: string` and the bound value is later read via ctx.get(as). Example builders.ts:443 `setVar('turnCount', (ctx) => (ctx.get('turnCount') ?? 0) + 1)`.
whyConcern: There is no compile-time relationship between a setVar/forEach key and the ctx.get('key') that reads it. A typo (`ctx.get('turnCout')`) silently returns undefined and the `?? 0` fallback masks it, so a counter silently resets every turn with no error — exactly the 'fallbacks masking real problems' anti-pattern CLAUDE.md warns about.
fixPaths: Consider a typed flow-state generic on FlowContext (e.g. FlowContext<G, Vars>) or at least a dev warning when get() is called for a key that was never set. flow/types.ts:54-64, flow/builders.ts:446-454.
verifierReasoning: Verified all cited locations. flow/types.ts:62-64 defines set/get with bare `string` keys and no type linkage. builders.ts:178-193 forEach uses `as: string`; builders.ts:446-454 setVar stores via ctx.set(name,...) with a string name; builders.ts:443 example uses `(ctx.get('turnCount') ?? 0) + 1`. The described failure mode is genuine: there is no compile-time relationship between a written key and a read key, so a typo'd ctx.get returns undefined, and a `?? default` fallback (idiomatic for counters) silently masks it instead of failing loud — which is exactly the anti-pattern CLAUDE.md warns about. However, impact is minor: the `?? 0` in the example is itself legitimate (the var truly doesn't exist on first iteration), so this is an API-shape/type-safety weakness rather than an active bug, and stringly-typed variable stores are a common, accepted pattern. Real, but low severity as claimed.

title: Per-action undo checkpoints accumulate unbounded full snapshots (O(N^2) memory)
evidence: runner.ts:69 actionCheckpoints never pruned; captureCheckpoint (107-112) keeps entries 0..len-1 forever, one per action. Each is a full createSnapshot (snapshot.ts:108-123): state=game.toJSON plus a copy of game.commandHistory (game.ts:891 only pushes, never trims). game-session.ts:1464 calls it on EVERY broadcast.
whyConcern: Long games hold hundreds of full deep state copies in memory; in a ~128MB Durable Object kept for the game lifetime this is an OOM/GC path, plus a full toJSON per action.
verifierReasoning: Verified every cited location. runner.ts:107-112 captureCheckpoint() does actionCheckpoints.slice(0,len) then sets [len], freezing [0..len-1] and growing one full snapshot per recorded action; actionHistory is whole-game (only trimmed on undo). snapshot.ts:111-122 createSnapshot copies state=game.toJSON() AND commandHistory=[...game.commandHistory] per entry. game.ts:885-895 execute() only pushes to commandHistory (the sole pop at 931 is MCTS undoLastCommand, not session play), so it grows O(N) over a game; copying it into each of N checkpoints gives O(N^2) total command-entry copies. game-session.ts:1464 calls captureCheckpoint() on every broadcast (the universal post-mutation funnel), so a full toJSON runs per action. Crucially, undo only ever consumes the current turn's checkpoint (state-history.ts:354-369; stateless-ops.ts:476-482) and slices to turnStartActionIndex+1 only after an undo — checkpoints below the current turn start are never pruned during forward play, so they are pure dead weight retained for the game lifetime in the in-memory runner. This is genuine unbounded accumulation: O(N) full state snapshots plus O(N^2) command-history copies, plus a full serialize per broadcast. The fix (cap retention at current turn start) is simple. The 'OOM' framing is slightly strong for short games but the scaling defect and avoidable cost are real, and heavy long-lived games (which they explicitly target) make it material.

title: Stateless op snapshot embeds the entire checkpoint array, persisted per op (O(N^2))
evidence: runner.ts:224-228 getSnapshot returns a snapshot carrying all N full per-action checkpoints. Each op calls fromSnapshot (runner.ts:330) then returns runner.getSnapshot (stateless-ops.ts:251), which the caller persists and passes to the next op. Each op reads N states and writes N+1.
whyConcern: Per-op payload and storage grow linearly with actions; O(N^2) total. KV/object-store value-size limits eventually hard-fail a game mid-session.
verifierReasoning: Verified at all cited locations. GameRunner.getSnapshot (runner.ts:224-228) returns the base snapshot plus a copy of actionCheckpoints. captureCheckpoint (runner.ts:107-112) only does slice(0, len) and sets [len] — it never prunes older checkpoints, so a game with N actions permanently carries N+1 checkpoints. Each checkpoint is a FULL snapshot from createSnapshot (snapshot.ts:103-123) embedding state: game.toJSON() (entire element tree) plus copies of commandHistory and actionHistory; snapshot.ts:52-62 confirms entries are non-nested but full-state. Every mutating handler in stateless-ops.ts (224/268/304/332/352/404) calls fromSnapshot then returns runner.getSnapshot (251/209/445 and via stateEnvelope at 142); executeOp is documented (700-705) as pure with the caller persisting OpResult.snapshot and passing it to the next op. Therefore each persisted snapshot is O(N) full game states (actually O(N·S + N²) since checkpoint[k] also carries k actions), and cumulative write volume across a game is O(N²·S). This is unmitigated; full retention serves authoritative undo (handleUndo 375-390) and dev-only debug time-travel (runnerFromCheckpoint 471-485). The hard-fail concern is real: per-snapshot size grows linearly with full game states, and Durable Object storage caps values at 128 KiB, so a long game could fail mid-session. Minor overstatement in the evidence: fromSnapshot performs one read of an object containing N states, not N separate reads — but the O(N) embedded-state-per-op and O(N²) total substance is correct.

title: HTTP action endpoint trusts client-supplied player identity (no authn) — act as any player
evidence: src/server/handlers/games.ts:199-216 `handleAction` does `await session.performAction(request.action, request.player, request.args)` — `request.player` comes straight from the POST body. src/session/game-session.ts:813-820 `performAction` only validates range: `if (player < 1 || player > this.#storedState.playerCount) return {... INVALID_PLAYER}` — it never checks the caller IS that player. The worker repeats this: src/worker/index.ts:1105-1118 `#handleAction` reads `const { action, player, args } = body` and calls `performAction(action, player, args)`.
whyConcern: There is no binding between the network caller and the `player` they claim to be. A `curl -X POST /games/<id>/action -d '{"action":"...","player":2,"args":{...}}'` submits moves on behalf of player 2. The WebSocket path correctly uses the server-tracked `session.playerSeat` (core.ts:505-509), but the HTTP route — shipped in both the standalone server and the production worker — bypasses that entirely.
fixPaths: src/server/handlers/games.ts (handleAction), src/worker/index.ts (#handleAction). Derive `player` from an authenticated session/token bound at connect/create time (e.g. a per-seat secret returned from claim-seat/join), not from request.player. Reject if the bound identity != requested player.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed in source. src/server/handlers/games.ts:209 — `handleAction` calls `session.performAction(request.action, request.player, request.args)` where `request.player` is taken directly from the POST body (route wired at core.ts:152-155). src/session/game-session.ts:813-820 — `performAction` only validates the player is in range (`player < 1 || player > playerCount`), with no binding to the network caller. The worker repeats the pattern: src/worker/index.ts:1115-1118 reads `const { action, player, args } = body` and passes `player` straight to `performAction` (and the public worker route at 415-431 forwards the raw body to the Durable Object). By contrast the WebSocket path at core.ts:505-509 correctly uses the server-tracked `session.playerSeat`, never a client-supplied value. I grepped src/server and src/worker for auth/token/bearer/cookie middleware and found none — there is no authentication layer wrapping these HTTP routes, so a `curl -X POST /games/<id>/action -d '{\"action\":...,\"player\":2,...}'` does submit moves as player 2. The vulnerability is real and shipped in both the standalone server and the production worker.\n\nSeverity: This is a genuine authorization/impersonation bypass — any client can act as any seat over HTTP. In the context of a board-game framework the concrete impact is cheating/impersonation (manipulating game outcomes), not RCE or PII exfiltration, and the codebase appears to lack a broader authn model generally (even WS seat assignment via matchmaking findIndex on a client-provided playerId is not strongly verified). Still, the HTTP path is strictly worse because the actor identity is fully client-controlled per request while the WS path at least anchors to a server-tracked seat. I rate it high rather than critical: it is a clear, real authz gap in production code, but its blast radius is game integrity within an active-development library rather than system/data compromise.

title: Hidden-information leak: GET state accepts arbitrary ?player= and returns that player's private view
evidence: src/server/handlers/games.ts:179-194 `handleGetGame(store, gameId, playerPosition)` uses `const player = playerPosition ?? 1; const result = session.getState(player)`; the value is parsed unauthenticated from the query in core.ts:147 `query.player ? parseInt(query.player, 10) : undefined`. getState -> buildPlayerState -> src/session/utils.ts:360 `const playerView = runner.getPlayerView(playerPosition)` which produces that player's privileged (hidden-info-revealing) view. Worker: src/worker/index.ts:591-594 + #handleGetState(player). The same unauthenticated player param feeds state-at (core.ts:170), state-diff (core.ts:180), pending-action (core.ts:225), action-traces (core.ts:189), and selection-choices (core.ts:281-287).
whyConcern: The engine's per-player filtering is correct, but the transport lets anyone pick whose perspective to render. `GET /games/<id>?player=2` returns player 2's hand/hidden cards to any caller. This defeats hidden information for every card/secret game over HTTP.
fixPaths: src/server/handlers/games.ts (handleGetGame/handleGetStateAt/handleGetStateDiff/handleGetPendingAction/handleGetActionTraces/handleGetSelectionChoices) and src/worker/index.ts equivalents: derive playerPosition from authenticated identity; spectators get position 0 (public view) only.
verifierReasoning:
Verified at the cited locations (paths corrected: handlers in src/server/handlers/games.ts, routing in src/server/core.ts, not core.ts under handlers). The claim is factually accurate.

- src/server/core.ts:147 parses `query.player` straight from the unauthenticated query string and passes it to handleGetGame. The same untrusted param feeds state-at (170), state-diff (180), action-traces (189), pending-action (225), and selection-choices reads `player` from the POST body (281-287).
- src/server/handlers/games.ts:190-191 `const player = playerPosition ?? 1; const result = session.getState(player)` — no check that the caller is that player.
- session.getState -> buildPlayerState (src/session/utils.ts:351-361) calls `runner.getPlayerView(playerPosition)`, which is the player-specific privileged view (the truthView includes that player's hidden info). The engine filtering is per the requested position, so requesting player=2 returns player 2's private view.
- src/worker/index.ts:591-594 has the identical pattern for the Cloudflare deployment target.

Critically, I searched for any auth layer (Authorization/Bearer/token/secret/player verification) across src/server/*.ts and src/worker/index.ts and found NONE. The server trusts client-supplied player identity everywhere — handleAction (core.ts:209) likewise forwards request.player unverified, so a caller can also act AS any player, not just read their view. So the hidden-info leak is one facet of a complete absence of an authentication/authorization model on the transport.

So `GET /games/<id>?player=2` does return player 2's hand/hidden cards to any caller. This genuinely defeats hidden information for any card/secret game served over HTTP.

Severity: I rate it high rather than critical. It is a real unauthenticated information-disclosure that breaks the core hidden-information guarantee, which would be critical for any publicly exposed deployment. I temper to high because (a) it reflects an across-the-board missing-auth posture (the action endpoint trusts client identity identically) rather than a single broken filter, consistent with an in-development library not yet shown to be deployed to untrusted users, and (b) the action-traces route even carries a comment acknowledging it's a debug endpoint. The fix is architectural: introduce per-player session/token auth so the transport binds the caller to a verified seat instead of trusting the query param. If this server is intended to be reachable by untrusted players as-is, critical is fully defensible.

title: WebSocket identity in the worker is self-asserted via URL params; playerId is also broadcast in lobby state
evidence: src/worker/index.ts:864-894 `#handleWebSocketUpgrade`: `const playerParam = url.searchParams.get('player'); const playerId = url.searchParams.get('playerId'); let playerPosition = playerParam ? parseInt(playerParam,10) : -1;` then if a playerId is given it is mapped via `getSeatForPlayer(playerId)` — but with no playerId at all it just trusts `?player=2`. There is no secret/token check. Meanwhile playerId is leaked to everyone: src/session/lobby-manager.ts:101-128 `getLobbyInfo()` returns `slots` directly, and slots carry `playerId` (set at lobby-manager.ts:196 `slot.playerId = playerId`). getLobbyInfo is broadcast to all clients (lobby-manager.ts:1014-1030) and returned by the lobby GET route.
whyConcern: Two independent impersonation paths: (1) connect with `?player=N` and you are seated as N with their private view and turn rights; (2) read the lobby (which echoes every player's playerId), then reconnect with `?playerId=<victim>` to be mapped to their seat. playerId is treated as a capability/secret elsewhere (host checks) yet is published to all clients.
fixPaths: src/worker/index.ts (#handleWebSocketUpgrade) and src/session/lobby-manager.ts (getLobbyInfo). Issue an unguessable per-seat token at claim/join and require it on WS connect + every mutating call; strip playerId (and any secret) from broadcast LobbyInfo, sending only a boolean 'isYou'/seat to each recipient.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed at all cited locations. In src/worker/index.ts:864-894 (`#handleWebSocketUpgrade`), identity is entirely self-asserted: `playerParam`/`?player=N` sets `playerPosition` directly (line 868) with no verification; `?playerId=X` is mapped to a seat via `getSeatForPlayer` (line 877) or `storedState.playerIds.indexOf` (line 882) with no secret/token check. A grep for token/secret/authoriz/authenticat in src/worker/index.ts returns nothing — there is no auth layer. The initial state sent back uses `getState(playerPosition)` (line 917), so an attacker passing `?player=N` receives victim N's private/hidden view and is seated with their turn rights. This is a genuine impersonation path.

The leak path is also real: LobbySlot carries `playerId` (src/types/protocol.ts:44), `getLobbyInfo()` returns `slots` directly plus `creatorId` (lobby-manager.ts:113-127), and `broadcastLobby()` sends the full LobbyInfo to every connected session (lobby-manager.ts:1014-1030). So every client learns every other player's playerId, including the creator's.

Crucially, playerId IS used as a capability elsewhere: host-only operations compare `playerId !== this.#storedState.creatorId` (lobby-manager.ts:412, 465, 540, 745, 884) for add/remove/toggle slots, kick, and modify others' options. Since creatorId is broadcast to all clients, any client can replay it to take over host privileges (kick players, reassign roles/colors).

Net effect: anonymous clients can (1) read any player's hidden game state and take their turns via `?player=N`/`?playerId=<victim>`, and (2) seize host control via the broadcast creatorId. This fully breaks the hidden-information and turn-integrity guarantees that are the entire point of a board-game engine. It is a real, present authorization bypass, not exaggerated. I rate it high rather than critical because this is a library-layer concern (a hosting platform could in principle wrap it with auth, and there is no PII/RCE), but as shipped the engine itself performs zero identity verification.

title: Lobby 'host only' authorization is bypassable: caller asserts its own playerId and creatorId is public
evidence: Host gates compare a client-supplied id to creatorId: src/session/lobby-manager.ts:412 `if (playerId !== this.#storedState.creatorId) return {... 'Only the host can add slots'}` (same pattern at :465 removeSlot, :540 setSlotAI, :745 kickPlayer, :884 updateSlotPlayerOptions, :942 updateGameOptions). The playerId arrives unauthenticated from the body (e.g. core.ts:346 `const { playerId } = body` for add-slot; worker/index.ts:1330-1345 likewise). creatorId is handed to every client: lobby-manager.ts:120 `creatorId: this.#storedState.creatorId` inside getLobbyInfo.
whyConcern: Any client reads the lobby, learns creatorId, then sends add-slot/remove-slot/set-slot-ai/kick-player/update-game-options with `playerId: <creatorId>` and passes the host check. The authorization is real in code but rests on an unverified, publicly-broadcast identifier.
fixPaths: src/session/lobby-manager.ts (all host-gated methods) + the handlers in src/server/core.ts and src/worker/index.ts that pass body.playerId. Bind host identity to the authenticated session token; remove creatorId from broadcast LobbyInfo.
verifierReasoning: Verified all cited locations in the actual source. Host-only authorization is implemented as a plain comparison of a client-supplied playerId against a publicly-broadcast creatorId. (1) Host gates: lobby-manager.ts:412 (addSlot), :465 (removeSlot), and "Verify caller is the host" checks at :744 (kickPlayer), :883 (updateSlotPlayerOptions), :941 (updateGameOptions) all do `playerId !== this.#storedState.creatorId`. (2) playerId is unauthenticated: core.ts:346/354/362/378/386/394/402 destructure playerId straight from the request body; the WS path uses session.playerId (types.ts:252, an optional field) which is likewise never verified against any credential. A grep across src/session and src/server for token/secret/jwt/authenticate/verify/cookie/Authorization returned nothing — there is no identity verification layer at all. (3) creatorId is leaked to every client: lobby-manager.ts:120 returns it inside getLobbyInfo(). (4) playerId is client-chosen: joinLobby (lobby-manager.ts:251) accepts any playerId the caller sends. Therefore any participant can read the lobby, copy creatorId, and submit it as their own playerId to pass every host check — enabling kicking players, flipping slots to AI, and mutating game options. The bypass is trivially exploitable. The root cause is actually broader: the whole identity model trusts client-asserted playerId, so players can also impersonate each other on non-host endpoints (setReady, updateName, leaveSeat). The finding is genuine and correctly described.

title: Unauthenticated restart wipes the game; create/action have no rate limiting or game-count cap (DoS)
evidence: src/server/handlers/games.ts:330-369 handleRestart takes only gameId, calls `store.deleteGame(gameId)` then recreates — no player/host check; routed at core.ts:299-303 with no identity. Worker mirrors this (worker/index.ts:635-639, #handleRestart at 1236). No rate limiting exists on any route (no middleware in core.ts handleRequest:119-471). createGame (handlers/games.ts:72) has no per-caller cap; the in-memory store grows unbounded.
whyConcern: Any client can POST /games/<id>/restart to destroy an in-progress game's state for all players (data loss). With no rate limiting, an attacker can spam /games to exhaust memory (in-memory/SQLite stores) or flood /action. WS messages are JSON.parsed (core.ts via handlers, worker/index.ts:948) with no size/type schema validation.
fixPaths: src/server/handlers/games.ts (handleRestart: require host/authenticated identity), src/server/core.ts (add rate limiting + max-games cap, validate WS message shape/size), src/worker/index.ts (#handleRestart auth).
verifierReasoning:
Verified all cited locations. handleRestart (src/server/handlers/games.ts:330-369) accepts only (store, registry, gameId, aiConfig) — no player/host/creator identity param — and unconditionally calls `store.deleteGame(gameId)` then `store.createGame` to rebuild from storedState. The route (src/server/core.ts:299-303) passes only the path-matched gameId, so any client that knows/guesses a gameId can destroy an in-progress game's state for all players. handleCreateGame (games.ts:72-174) generates a fresh gameId on every POST /games with no per-caller cap, and grep confirms NO rate-limiting code exists anywhere in src/server or src/worker. So the DoS (unbounded memory growth via /games spam, /action flooding) and data-loss (unauthenticated restart) claims are both genuinely present in the code.

Important context that shapes severity: this is not a restart-specific gap — the ENTIRE server has no authentication/authorization model. /action, /undo, the debug deck-manipulation routes (move-to-top, shuffle, transfer), and rewind are all equally unauthenticated, and lobby endpoints trust a self-asserted playerId from the body rather than any verified token. So an attacker can already submit moves as any player via /action, which is at least as damaging as restart. The finding correctly identifies a real, systemic trust-the-client design rather than an isolated bug. Impact is griefing/data-loss within game sessions and memory exhaustion — no sensitive-data exposure, and this is an actively-developed library where deployment-layer auth may be expected. Medium is the appropriate severity.

title: Snapshot/restore/checkpoint sprawl: 5 restore paths + 2 unrelated checkpoint systems, no single source of truth
evidence: Restore mechanisms: (1) GameRunner.fromSnapshot — state-authoritative (runner.ts:303); (2) GameRunner.replay — action replay (runner.ts:261); (3) captureDevState/restoreDevState — HMR transfer (game-session.ts:#reloadWithDevTransfer ~948-1060); (4) Game.restoreGame/loadSerializedState (game.ts:2660,2612); (5) #reloadFromCheckpoint partial replay (game-session.ts:1132). Two SEPARATE checkpoint systems: runner.actionCheckpoints — per-action, carried inside the snapshot, used for undo (runner.ts:64-111) AND CheckpointManager — 'every N actions, kept in memory, not persisted' for HMR (checkpoint-manager.ts:1-9,57-69). game-session.ts reloadWithCurrentRules (870-936) chains dev-transfer → checkpoint partial-replay → full replay as cascading fallbacks.
whyConcern: There is no canonical answer to 'how is a game reconstructed from saved state.' Each path has different soundness (replay unsound, fromSnapshot authoritative, dev-transfer schema-validated, partial-replay hybrid) and different inputs (action history vs element JSON vs per-action checkpoints vs every-N checkpoints). The two checkpoint systems share the word 'checkpoint' but are unrelated, which is a naming/maintenance trap.
fixPaths: Collapse to one authoritative restore primitive (fromSnapshot) and express undo/rewind/time-travel/HMR as 'produce a snapshot, then fromSnapshot.' Unify the two checkpoint stores or rename one (e.g. runner.undoCheckpoints vs CheckpointManager.hmrCheckpoints). Make replay a derive-snapshot helper, not a parallel restore path.
verifierReasoning: The cited code structures all exist as described, but the "high-severity sprawl / no single source of truth" framing is exaggerated. Verified each citation: (1) fromSnapshot (runner.ts:303) is the authoritative, dominant production path — 15+ uses in stateless-ops.ts, whose header explicitly declares itself "the single source of truth for running one BoardSmith game operation." (2) Game.restoreGame/loadSerializedState (game.ts:2660/2612) are NOT independent restore paths — loadSerializedState is the primitive fromSnapshot calls (runner.ts:336), and restoreGame is a thin static wrapper (MCTS clone). Counting them separately double-counts layers. (3) GameRunner.replay (runner.ts:261) is used by GameSession.restore (game-session.ts:539) only because StoredGameState persists action history, not snapshots — a different storage format that legitimately requires replay — plus time-travel debugging in getStateAtAction (state-history.ts:114). (4) #reloadWithDevTransfer and #reloadFromCheckpoint are dev-only HMR fallbacks forming one deliberate, documented cascade (dev-transfer -> checkpoint partial-replay -> full replay, game-session.ts:970-1000). So there is a clear per-domain hierarchy with documented canonical answers, not undifferentiated sprawl; the comments even explain why replay was abandoned inside fromSnapshot for soundness. The only genuinely fair point is the naming overlap: runner.actionCheckpoints (per-action undo, carried in snapshot, runner.ts:64-111) vs CheckpointManager (every-N, in-memory, dev-only HMR, checkpoint-manager.ts:1-9) are unrelated mechanisms sharing the word "checkpoint" — a minor maintainability nit, mitigated by living in different modules with distinct documented purposes. No correctness bug is demonstrated; the architecture is intentional and heavily commented. Real as an observation, but overstated; effectively a low-severity naming/cleanliness concern.

title: Game is a 2684-LOC god object spanning ~10 responsibilities and inheriting the full Space container API
evidence: src/engine/element/game.ts is 2684 lines; `export class Game extends Space` (game.ts:286-289). ~88 public/protected methods cover: element registry (getElementById:862, registerElements:825), command exec/undo (execute:885, replayCommands:900, undoLastCommand:918, undoCommands:944), action registry+exec (registerAction:962, getActionExecutor:1027, getSelectionChoices:1035), flow control (setFlow:1499, startFlow:1513, continueFlow:1538, restoreFlowState:1611), RNG (getRandomState:1632,setRandomState:1641), player mgmt (players:1755, nextPlayer:1964, setCurrentPlayer:1923), serialization (toJSON:2445, loadSerializedState:2612, restoreGame:2660), debugging (debugElementTree:1216, debugAllActions:1193), animation buffer (animate:2404, pushAnimationEvent:2414), and the dev-only auto-sync proxy machinery (519-721).
whyConcern: Every concern in the engine routes through one class that designers subclass. Because Game extends Space, a designer's game subclass also exposes the entire container/query surface (create/all/first/...) intermixed with lifecycle and flow APIs, blurring 'what am I supposed to override' vs 'what is internal plumbing.'
fixPaths: Extract cohesive collaborators already implied by the method clusters (FlowController, RandomSource, ActionRegistry, Serializer, DebugInspector) and compose them on Game; keep the designer-facing surface small. Consider not having Game extend Space (use composition for the root container).
verifierReasoning: Every factual claim checks out against the source. src/engine/element/game.ts is exactly 2684 lines (wc -l). `export class Game<G,P> extends Space<G,P>` confirmed at game.ts:286-289. All ~24 cited method locations are accurate to the line: getElementById:862, execute:885, replayCommands:900, undoLastCommand:918, undoCommands:944, registerAction:962, getActionExecutor:1027, getSelectionChoices:1035, debugAllActions:1193, debugElementTree:1216, setFlow:1499, startFlow:1513, continueFlow:1538, restoreFlowState:1611, getRandomState:1632, setRandomState:1641, get players:1755, setCurrentPlayer:1923, nextPlayer:1964, animate:2404, pushAnimationEvent:2415, override toJSON:2445, loadSerializedState:2612, registerElements:825. The dev-only proxy machinery (_checkForVolatileState/createSyncProxy) is real in the 519-721 range. The class does cohabit element-registry, command exec/undo, action registry, flow control, RNG, player mgmt, serialization, debugging, and animation concerns, and via `extends Space` designer subclasses do inherit the full container surface (create/all/first/etc). So the description is accurate, not exaggerated.\n\nHowever, this is a maintainability/architecture observation, not a defect: nothing here causes incorrect behavior, data loss, or security issues. Two points temper the claimed 'medium' severity: (1) `Game extends Space` is an intentional and idiomatic design for board-game engines — the game IS the root container/space, so inheriting the container API is by design, not accidental leakage; (2) the responsibilities are documented with extensive JSDoc and the override surface is signposted (static playerView/PlayerClass, registerElements, setFlow). The strongest legitimate extraction candidate is the dev-only proxy/volatile-state machinery (519-721), which is genuinely orthogonal plumbing. Real concern, but its impact is stylistic/long-term-maintenance rather than functional, so I rate it low.
